title: Transition Metal Dichalcogenide Excitons in Periodic Electrostatic Potentials: Center-of-Mass Models
authors: Jose M. Torres-Lopez, Sudipta Kundu, Felipe H. da Jornada, Tony Heinz, Allan H. MacDonald
abstract: Two-dimensional (2D) van-der-Waals materials are a promising platform for exciton state engi- neering. In this paper, we study the properties of excitons in 2D group VI transition-metal dichalco- genide (TMD) semiconductors that are modified by a periodic electrostatic potential through the quadratic Stark effect. Using a model that retains only center-of-mass and valley degrees-of-freedom, we find that electrostatic potentials can drive optical valley splitting up to 10meVs and induce valley selective exciton dispersion. We explain why both properties are sensitive to the rotational symmetry of the electrostatic trapping potential using a combination of numerical results and an- alytical approximations. An important consequence of valley-splitting is that the lowest exciton band is non-degenerate and has a linear dispersion around gamma that is expected to suppress thermal excitations, allowing true Bose condensation and superfluidity of excitons in two space dimensions.
summary: This paper explores how periodic electrostatic landscapes can be used to engineer the valley properties of excitons in 2D TMDs. It demonstrates that breaking rotational symmetry can lift valley degeneracy and create linear dispersion. These findings suggest a method to stabilize 2D exciton superfluids by suppressing thermal excitations.
main_problem: Investigating how periodic electrostatic potentials and reduced rotational symmetry affect the exciton spectra, valley degeneracy, and dispersion in 2D transition-metal dichalcogenide (TMD) semiconductors.
main_result: Electrostatic potentials can drive optical valley splitting up to 10 meV and induce valley-selective dispersion, where the lowest exciton band becomes non-degenerate with linear dispersion, potentially enabling 2D Bose condensation.
method: The study uses a center-of-mass model with exact diagonalization, analytical perturbation theory, and a tight-binding/Wannier representation using Gaussian wavefunctions.
model_or_system: 2D group VI TMD semiconductors subjected to periodic electrostatic potentials (triangular, square, or striped) via the quadratic Stark effect, modeled using a Hamiltonian containing kinetic energy, periodic potential, and electron-hole exchange interaction.
key_observables: Optical valley splitting (valley gap), exciton band dispersion (linear vs. quadratic), and optical conductivity/absorption weight.
important_parameters: Potential strength (approx. 1 meV), lattice constant (approx. 50 nm), exchange interaction strength (0.4-0.6 eV nm), and anisotropy parameter (alpha).
main_assumptions: The smooth potential limit (weak potential gradients), the local quadratic Stark shift approximation, and the truncation of the exchange interaction to the first shell of reciprocal lattice vectors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows reciprocal lattice vectors and exciton bands for triangular and square lattices; Figure 2 compares isotropic vs. anisotropic triangular potentials; Figure 3 shows exciton band structures for triangular lattices; Figure 4 compares square and anisotropic potentials.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and the center-of-mass model, analyzes the effect of rotational symmetry on valley splitting and dispersion, explores the impact of anisotropy on band stability, and discusses the implications for 2D Bose condensation.
why_interesting: The discovery that engineered potentials can create a non-degenerate, linearly dispersing exciton band provides a theoretical pathway to achieving true 2D Bose-Einstein condensation and superfluidity, which is otherwise suppressed by thermal fluctuations in standard 2D systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Top: Vectors in the first shell of the reciprocal lat- tice (blue arrows) and Brillouin zone (BZ) (grey area) for the triangular (left) and square lattices (right). Bottom: Six lowest exchange-free bands computed for ∆j = 1 meV on the first shell, exchange constant J = 0 and lattice constant a = 50 nm. The states always occur in valley-degenerate pairs. Anticipating the anisotropy introduced later we have distinguished the BZ edge center (m) points as m1, m2, ... following the notation for the first-shell Gj vectors. The bands are then plotted along the path k −m1 −γ −m2 −k illustrated in red.

Figure 2. Triangular exciton potential: C3−symmetric case with α = 1 (left) and asymmetric case with parameter α = 0.6 (right). For clarity, the unit cell is marked by the black parallelogram and the potential minima (maxima) are encircled by the black (white) dashed lines. The coordinate system has been shifted to place a potential minimum at a(2, 0)/ √

Figure 4. Square lattice potential for the C4−symmetric case with α = 1 (left) and for anisotropy parameter α = 0.6 (right). The unit cell is marked by the black square, and the potential potential minima (maxima) are encircled by the black (white) dashed lines. The coordinate system has been shifted to place the potential minimum at a(3/4, 1/4).
Figure 3. The six lowest-energy bands for the isotropic (α = 1, left) and anisotropic (α = 0.6, right) triangular lattice, plotted along the path defined in Fig. 1.
Figure 5. Six lowest energy exciton bands for the isotropic (α = 1, left) and anisotropic (α = 0.6, right) square lattices.
